title: Obermann
content:
آ آ آ آ In front the awful Alpine track
آ آ آ آ Crawls up its rocky stair;
آ آ آ آ The autumn storm-winds drive the rack
آ آ آ آ Close oï؟½er it, in the air.

آ آ آ آ Behind are the abandonï؟½d baths
آ آ آ آ Mute in their meadows lone;
آ آ آ آ The leaves are on the valley paths;
آ آ آ آ The mists are on the Rhone,

آ آ آ آ The white mists rolling like a sea.
آ آ آ آ I hear the torrents roar.
آ آ آ آ Yes, Obermann, all speaks of thee!
آ آ آ آ I feel thee near once more.

آ آ آ آ I turn thy leaves: I feel their breath
آ آ آ آ Once more upon me roll;
آ آ آ آ That air of languor, cold, and death,
آ آ آ آ Which brooded oï؟½er thy soul.

آ آ آ آ Fly hence, poor Wretch, whoeï؟½er thou art,
آ آ آ آ Condemnï؟½d to cast about,
آ آ آ آ All shipwreck in thy own weak heart,
آ آ آ آ For comfort from without:

آ آ آ آ A fever in these pages burns
آ آ آ آ Beneath the calm they feign;
آ آ آ آ A wounded human spirit turns
آ آ آ آ Here, on its bed of pain.

آ آ آ آ Yes, though the virgin mountain air
آ آ آ آ Fresh through these pages blows,
آ آ آ آ Though to these leaves the glaciers spare
آ آ آ آ The soul of their white snows,

آ آ آ آ Though here a mountain murmur swells
آ آ آ آ Of many a dark-boughï؟½d pine,
آ آ آ آ Though, as you read, you hear the bells
آ آ آ آ Of the high-pasturing kine,

آ آ آ آ Yet, through the hum of torrent lone,
آ آ آ آ And brooding mountain bee,
آ آ آ آ There sobs I know not what ground tone
آ آ آ آ Of human agony.

آ آ آ آ Is it for this, because the sound
آ آ آ آ Is fraught too deep with pain,
آ آ آ آ That, Obermann! the world around
آ آ آ آ So little loves thy strain?

آ آ آ آ Some secrets may the poet tell,
آ آ آ آ For the world loves new ways.
آ آ آ آ To tell too deep ones is not well;
آ آ آ آ It knows not what he says.

آ آ آ آ Yet of the spirits who have reignï؟½d
آ آ آ آ In this our troubled day,
آ آ آ آ I know but two, who have attainï؟½d,
آ آ آ آ Save thee, to see their way.

آ آ آ آ By Englandï؟½s lakes, in grey old age,
آ آ آ آ His quiet home one keeps;
آ آ آ آ And one, the strong much-toiling Sage,
آ آ آ آ In German Weimar sleeps.

آ آ آ آ But Wordsworthï؟½s eyes avert their ken
آ آ آ آ From half of human fate;
آ آ آ آ And Goetheï؟½s course few sons of men
آ آ آ آ May think to emulate.

آ آ آ آ For he pursued a lonely road,
آ آ آ آ His eyes on Natureï؟½s plan;
آ آ آ آ Neither made man too much a God,
آ آ آ آ Nor God too much a man.

آ آ آ آ Strong was he, with a spirit free
آ آ آ آ From mists, and sane, and clear;
آ آ آ آ Clearer, how much! than ours: yet we
آ آ آ آ Have a worse course to steer.

آ آ آ آ For though his manhood bore the blast
آ آ آ آ Of Europeï؟½s stormiest time,
آ آ آ آ Yet in a tranquil world was passï؟½d
آ آ آ آ His tenderer youthful prime.

آ آ آ آ But we, brought forth and rearï؟½d in hours
آ آ آ آ Of change, alarm, surprise,
آ آ آ آ What shelter to grow ripe is ours?
آ آ آ آ What leisure to grow wiseï؟½?

آ آ آ آ Like children bathing on the shore,
آ آ آ آ Buried a wave beneath,
آ آ آ آ The second wave succeeds, before
آ آ آ آ We have had time to breathe.

آ آ آ آ Too fast we live, too much are tried,
آ آ آ آ Too harassï؟½d, to attain
آ آ آ آ Wordsworthï؟½s sweet calm, or Goetheï؟½s wide
آ آ آ آ And luminous view to gain.

آ آ آ آ And then we turn, thou sadder Sage!
آ آ آ آ To thee: we feel thy spell.
آ آ آ آ The hopeless tangle of our age,
آ آ آ آ Thou too hast scannï؟½d it well.

آ آ آ آ Immovable thou sittest; still
آ آ آ آ As death; composï؟½d to bear.
آ آ آ آ Thy head is clear, thy feeling chill,
آ آ آ آ And icy thy despair.

آ آ آ آ Yes, as the Son of Thetis said,
آ آ آ آ One hears thee saying now,
آ آ آ آ Greater by far than thou are dead:
آ آ آ آ Strive not: die also thou.,

آ آ آ آ Ah! Two desires toss about
آ آ آ آ The poetï؟½s feverish blood.
آ آ آ آ One drives him to the world without,
آ آ آ آ And one to solitude.

آ آ آ آ The glow, he cries, the thrill of life,
آ آ آ آ Where, where do these abound?
آ آ آ آ Not in the world, not in the strife
آ آ آ آ Of men, shall they be found.

آ آ آ آ He who hath watchï؟½d, not sharï؟½d, the strife,
آ آ آ آ Knows how the day hath gone;
آ آ آ آ He only lives with the worldï؟½s life
آ آ آ آ Who hath renouncï؟½d his own.

آ آ آ آ To thee we come, then. Clouds are rollï؟½d
آ آ آ آ Where thou, O Seer, art set;
آ آ آ آ Thy realm of thought is drear and cold,
آ آ آ آ The world is colder yet!

آ آ آ آ And thou hast pleasures too to share
آ آ آ آ With those who come to thee:
آ آ آ آ Balms floating on thy mountain air,
آ آ آ آ And healing sights to see.

آ آ آ آ How often, where the slopes are green
آ آ آ آ On Jaman, hast thou sate
آ آ آ آ By some high chalet door, and seen
آ آ آ آ The summer day grow late,

آ آ آ آ And darkness steal oï؟½er the wet grass
آ آ آ آ With the pale crocus starrï؟½d,
آ آ آ آ And reach that glimmering sheet of glass
آ آ آ آ Beneath the piny sward,

آ آ آ آ Lake Lemanï؟½s waters, far below:
آ آ آ آ And watchï؟½d the rosy light
آ آ آ آ Fade from the distant peaks of snow:
آ آ آ آ And on the air of night

آ آ آ آ Heard accents of the eternal tongue
آ آ آ آ Through the pine branches play:
آ آ آ آ Listenï؟½d. and felt thyself grow young;
آ آ آ آ Listenï؟½d, and wept Away!

آ آ آ آ Away the dreams that but deceive
آ آ آ آ And thou, sad Guide, adieu!
آ آ آ آ I go; Fate drives me: but I leave
آ آ آ آ Half of my life with you.

آ آ آ آ We, in some unknown Powerï؟½s employ,
آ آ آ آ Move on a rigorous line:
آ آ آ آ Can neither, when we will, enjoy;
آ آ آ آ Nor, when we will, resign.

آ آ آ آ I in the world must live: but thou,
آ آ آ آ Thou melancholy Shade!
آ آ آ آ Wilt not, if thou canst see me now,
آ آ آ آ Condemn me, nor upbraid.

آ آ آ آ For thou art gone away from earth,
آ آ آ آ And place with those dost claim,
آ آ آ آ The Children of the Second Birth
آ آ آ آ Whom the world could not tame;

آ آ آ آ And with that small transfigurï؟½d Band,
آ آ آ آ Whom many a different way
آ آ آ آ Conducted to their common land,
آ آ آ آ Thou learnï؟½st to think as they.

آ آ آ آ Christian and pagan, king and slave,
آ آ آ آ Soldier and anchorite,
آ آ آ آ Distinctions we esteem so grave,
آ آ آ آ Are nothing in their sight.

آ آ آ آ They do not ask, who pinï؟½d unseen,
آ آ آ آ Who was on action hurlï؟½d,
آ آ آ آ Whose one bond is that all have been
آ آ آ آ Unspotted by the world.

آ آ آ آ There without anger thou wilt see
آ آ آ آ Him who obeys thy spell
آ آ آ آ No more, so he but rest, like thee,
آ آ آ آ Unsoilï؟½d:, and so, Farewell

آ آ آ آ Farewell!, Whether thou now liest near
آ آ آ آ That much-lovï؟½d inland sea,
آ آ آ آ The ripples of whose blue waves cheer
آ آ آ آ Vevey and Meillerie,

آ آ آ آ And in that gracious region bland,
آ آ آ آ Where with clear-rustling wave
آ آ آ آ The scented pines of Switzerland
آ آ آ آ Stand dark round thy green grave,

آ آ آ آ Between the dusty vineyard walls
آ آ آ آ Issuing on that green place
آ آ آ آ The early peasant still recalls
آ آ آ آ The pensive strangerï؟½s face,

آ آ آ آ And stoops to clear thy moss-grown date
آ آ آ آ Ere he plods on again;
آ آ آ آ Or whether, by maligner Fate,
آ آ آ آ Among the swarms of men,

آ آ آ آ Where between granite terraces
آ آ آ آ The blue Seine rolls her wave,
آ آ آ آ The Capital of Pleasure sees
آ آ آ آ Thy hardly-heard-of grave,

آ آ آ آ Farewell! Under the sky we part,
آ آ آ آ In this stern Alpine dell.
آ آ آ آ O unstrung will! O broken heart!
آ آ آ آ A last, a last farewell!
meaning:
About: Matthew Arnold (24 December 1822 ï؟½ 15 April 1888) was an English poet and cultural critic, who worked as an inspector of schools. 
He was the son of Thomas Arnold, the famed headmaster of Rugby School, and brother to Tom Arnold, literary professor, and William Delafield Arnold, novelist and colonial administrator.
